content: A black hole is a region in space where gravity is so powerful that nothing, not even light, can escape. Imagine a whirlpool in a river, where anything that gets too close is pulled in and can’t get out. Black holes form when massive stars collapse under their own weight, squeezing a huge amount of matter into an incredibly small area. This creates an intense gravitational pull. The boundary around a black hole, known as the event horizon, marks the point of no return—once crossed, escape is impossible. Because light itself cannot break free, black holes appear completely dark, making them invisible against the backdrop of space. Understanding black holes helps scientists explore the limits of physics and the nature of our universe. Despite their mysterious reputation, black holes are natural cosmic objects that reveal the extraordinary power of gravity in shaping the cosmos.
